title: Choosing a File Format
concept: Choosing an image format is a practical decision: JPEG suits photographs where small size matters more than perfect fidelity, PNG suits graphics and transparency, and machine learning datasets often prefer lossless formats so training data stays exact and unbiased.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is PNG's alpha channel used for?

options.text: Controlling how transparent or opaque each pixel is
Making the file compress using lossy methods
Storing the image as vector shapes instead of pixels

exercises.explain: Right — the alpha channel is PNG's per-pixel transparency control.

cards.title: A toolbox, not one "best" tool
cards.body: By now you know raster vs. vector, lossy vs. lossless, and how JPEG and PNG each work. The real skill isn't memorizing which format is "best" — it's picking the right tool for the job, the same way a photographer picks a different toolbox item for landscapes than for portraits.

cards.title: The format decision
cards.body: Choosing a file format means weighing tradeoffs for a specific use case: photograph with complex colors → JPEG (lossy, small size). Graphic, icon, or anything needing transparency → PNG (lossless, alpha channel). Simple logo that must resize to any size → vector format like SVG.

cards.title: Packing for the right trip
cards.body: You wouldn't pack the same bag for a weekend camping trip and a business conference. Likewise, you wouldn't use the same file format for a sprawling vacation photo album and a set of sharp-edged app icons — each job has a format that fits it best.

cards.title: Why ML datasets often lean lossless
cards.body: A team building an image classifier wants every training photo to reflect real, unaltered pixel data — JPEG artifacts introduced at different compression levels could quietly bias what the model learns to recognize. That's why many computer vision datasets store images losslessly (like PNG or raw formats), especially in early collection stages, even though this means larger files.

cards.title: "Just always use PNG to be safe"
cards.body: Defaulting to PNG for everything sounds cautious, but it isn't free — a full-color photo saved as PNG can be five to ten times larger than the same photo as JPEG, for a fidelity difference most viewers will never notice. The safe choice is picking the format that matches the image's content and purpose.

cards.title: Quick Format Picker
cards.body: A simple mental shortcut for choosing:

left.text:
Millions of subtle colors
Small size matters
→ JPEG

right.text:
Sharp edges, few colors, or needs transparency
Exactness matters
→ PNG

exercises.prompt: Why might a computer vision team prefer lossless image formats for their training dataset?

options.text: Lossy artifacts at inconsistent compression levels could quietly bias what the model learns
Lossless files are always smaller than lossy files
Lossy formats cannot be opened by machine learning code at all
Lossless is required by law for all datasets

exercises.explain: Correct — unpredictable compression artifacts could introduce noise or bias, so many datasets favor exact, lossless pixel data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PNG is always the objectively correct choice for every type of image, including photographs.
exercises.explain: False. For photographs, JPEG's much smaller file size is usually worth its minor, often unnoticeable quality tradeoff.

exercises.sentence: A photo with millions of subtle colors is usually best stored as ___, while a logo needing transparency is usually best stored as PNG.
exercises.explain: JPEG is correct — its lossy compression handles complex photographic color detail efficiently at a small file size.

exercises.prompt: Which choice best reflects sound file-format decision-making?

options.text: Save a sunset photo as JPEG for a small file, and a transparent app icon as PNG
Save every single image, photo or icon, as the exact same format for consistency
Pick a format at random each time an image is saved

exercises.explain: Matching the format to the content — JPEG for the photo, PNG for the transparent icon — is the sound, deliberate choice.

exercises.prompt: A startup is building an app: it needs vacation photos in the gallery, a transparent logo in the header, and a labeled image dataset to train a classifier. Which formats fit each need, and why?

options.text: JPEG for the vacation photos (small size, photographic detail), PNG for the transparent logo (lossless with alpha channel), and a lossless format for the training dataset (avoids compression artifacts biasing the model)
JPEG for everything, since smaller files are always better regardless of purpose
PNG for everything, since lossless is always the safest choice no matter the tradeoff
Vector format for the photos and dataset, since vector always outperforms raster

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Matching format to purpose — JPEG for photographic content, PNG for transparency and exactness, lossless for training data integrity — is the core skill of this whole unit. You're ready to move from these classical image fundamentals toward how computer vision models actually process pixels.
